html:
X

Scouts Out

‚ÄúAnd so, Miss Wren,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEugene Wrayburn, ‚ÄúI cannot persuade you to dress me a doll?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù replied Miss Wren snappishly; ‚Äúif you want one, go and buy one at the shop.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd my charming young goddaughter,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝWrayburn plaintively, ‚Äúdown in Hertfordshire‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

(‚ÄúHumbugshire you mean, I think,‚Äù interposed Miss Wren.)

‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚Äîis to be put upon the cold footing of the general public, and is to derive no advantage from my private acquaintance with the Court Dressmaker?‚Äù

‚ÄúIf it‚Äôs any advantage to your charming godchild‚ÅÝ‚Äîand oh, a precious godfather she has got!‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîreplied Miss Wren, pricking at him in the air with her needle, ‚Äúto be informed that the Court Dressmaker knows your tricks and your manners, you may tell her so by post, with my compliments.‚Äù

Miss Wren was busy at her work by candlelight, and Mr.¬ÝWrayburn, half amused and half vexed, and all idle and shiftless, stood by her bench looking on. Miss Wren‚Äôs troublesome child was in the corner in deep disgrace, and exhibiting great wretchedness in the shivering stage of prostration from drink.

‚ÄúUgh, you disgraceful boy!‚Äù exclaimed Miss Wren, attracted by the sound of his chattering teeth, ‚ÄúI wish they‚Äôd all drop down your throat and play at dice in your stomach! Boh, wicked child! Bee-baa, black sheep!‚Äù

On her accompanying each of these reproaches with a threatening stamp of the foot, the wretched creature protested with a whine.

‚ÄúPay five shillings for you indeed!‚Äù Miss Wren proceeded; ‚Äúhow many hours do you suppose it costs me to earn five shillings, you infamous boy?‚ÅÝ‚ÄîDon‚Äôt cry like that, or I‚Äôll throw a doll at you. Pay five shillings fine for you indeed. Fine in more ways than one, I think! I‚Äôd give the dustman five shillings, to carry you off in the dust cart.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, no,‚Äù pleaded the absurd creature. ‚ÄúPlease!‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs enough to break his mother‚Äôs heart, is this boy,‚Äù said Miss Wren, half appealing to Eugene. ‚ÄúI wish I had never brought him up. He‚Äôd be sharper than a serpent‚Äôs tooth, if he wasn‚Äôt as dull as ditch water. Look at him. There‚Äôs a pretty object for a parent‚Äôs eyes!‚Äù

Assuredly, in his worse than swinish state (for swine at least fatten on their guzzling, and make themselves good to eat), he was a pretty object for any eyes.

‚ÄúA muddling and a swipey old child,‚Äù said Miss Wren, rating him with great severity, ‚Äúfit for nothing but to be preserved in the liquor that destroys him, and put in a great glass bottle as a sight for other swipey children of his own pattern‚ÅÝ‚Äîif he has no consideration for his liver, has he none for his mother?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. Deration, oh don‚Äôt!‚Äù cried the subject of these angry remarks.

‚ÄúOh don‚Äôt and oh don‚Äôt,‚Äù pursued Miss Wren. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs oh do and oh do. And why do you?‚Äù

‚ÄúWon‚Äôt do so any more. Won‚Äôt indeed. Pray!‚Äù

‚ÄúThere!‚Äù said Miss Wren, covering her eyes with her hand. ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt bear to look at you. Go upstairs and get me my bonnet and shawl. Make yourself useful in some way, bad boy, and let me have your room instead of your company, for one half minute.‚Äù

Obeying her, he shambled out, and Eugene Wrayburn saw the tears exude from between the little creature‚Äôs fingers as she kept her hand before her eyes. He was sorry, but his sympathy did not move his carelessness to do anything but feel sorry.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm going to the Italian Opera to try on,‚Äù said Miss Wren, taking away her hand after a little while, and laughing satirically to hide that she had been crying; ‚ÄúI must see your back before I go, Mr.¬ÝWrayburn. Let me first tell you, once for all, that it‚Äôs of no use your paying visits to me. You wouldn‚Äôt get what you want, of me, no, not if you brought pincers with you to tear it out.‚Äù

‚ÄúAre you so obstinate on the subject of a doll‚Äôs dress for my godchild?‚Äù

‚ÄúAh!‚Äù returned Miss Wren with a hitch of her chin, ‚ÄúI am so obstinate. And of course it‚Äôs on the subject of a doll‚Äôs dress‚ÅÝ‚Äîor address‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhichever you like. Get along and give it up!‚Äù

Her degraded charge had come back, and was standing behind her with the bonnet and shawl.

‚ÄúGive ‚Äôem to me and get back into your corner, you naughty old thing!‚Äù said Miss Wren, as she turned and espied him. ‚ÄúNo, no, I won‚Äôt have your help. Go into your corner, this minute!‚Äù

The miserable man, feebly rubbing the back of his faltering hands downward from the wrists, shuffled on to his post of disgrace; but not without a curious glance at Eugene in passing him, accompanied with what seemed as if it might have been an action of his elbow, if any action of any limb or joint he had, would have answered truly to his will. Taking no more particular notice of him than instinctively falling away from the disagreeable contact, Eugene, with a lazy compliment or so to Miss Wren, begged leave to light his cigar, and departed.

‚ÄúNow you prodigal old son,‚Äù said Jenny, shaking her head and her emphatic little forefinger at her burden, ‚Äúyou sit there till I come back. You dare to move out of your corner for a single instant while I‚Äôm gone, and I‚Äôll know the reason why.‚Äù

With this admonition, she blew her work candles out, leaving him to the light of the fire, and, taking her big door-key in her pocket and her crutch-stick in her hand, marched off.

Eugene lounged slowly towards the Temple, smoking his cigar, but saw no more of the dolls‚Äô dressmaker, through the accident of their taking opposite sides of the street. He lounged along moodily, and stopped at Charing Cross to look about him, with as little interest in the crowd as any man might take, and was lounging on again, when a most unexpected object caught his eyes. No less an object than Jenny Wren‚Äôs bad boy trying to make up his mind to cross the road.

A more ridiculous and feeble spectacle than this tottering wretch making unsteady sallies into the roadway, and as often staggering back again, oppressed by terrors of vehicles that were a long way off or were nowhere, the streets could not have shown. Over and over again, when the course was perfectly clear, he set out, got halfway, described a loop, turned, and went back again; when he might have crossed and recrossed half a dozen times. Then, he would stand shivering on the edge of the pavement, looking up the street and looking down, while scores of people jostled him, and crossed, and went on. Stimulated in course of time by the sight of so many successes, he would make another sally, make another loop, would all but have his foot on the opposite pavement, would see or imagine something coming, and would stagger back again. There, he would stand making spasmodic preparations as if for a great leap, and at last would decide on a start at precisely the wrong moment, and would be roared at by drivers, and would shrink back once more, and stand in the old spot shivering, with the whole of the proceedings to go through again.

‚ÄúIt strikes me,‚Äù remarked Eugene coolly, after watching him for some minutes, ‚Äúthat my friend is likely to be rather behind time if he has any appointment on hand.‚Äù With which remark he strolled on, and took no further thought of him.

Lightwood was at home when he got to the Chambers, and had dined alone there. Eugene drew a chair to the fire by which he was having his wine and reading the evening paper, and brought a glass, and filled it for good fellowship‚Äôs sake.

‚ÄúMy dear Mortimer, you are the express picture of contented industry, reposing (on credit) after the virtuous labours of the day.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear Eugene, you are the express picture of discontented idleness not reposing at all. Where have you been?‚Äù

‚ÄúI have been,‚Äù replied Wrayburn, ‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚Äîabout town. I have turned up at the present juncture, with the intention of consulting my highly intelligent and respected solicitor on the position of my affairs.‚Äù

‚ÄúYour highly intelligent and respect solicitor is of opinion that your affairs are in a bad way, Eugene.‚Äù

‚ÄúThough whether,‚Äù said Eugene thoughtfully, ‚Äúthat can be intelligently said, now, of the affairs of a client who has nothing to lose and who cannot possibly be made to pay, may be open to question.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou have fallen into the hands of the Jews, Eugene.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear boy,‚Äù returned the debtor, very composedly taking up his glass, ‚Äúhaving previously fallen into the hands of some of the Christians, I can bear it with philosophy.‚Äù

‚ÄúI have had an interview today, Eugene, with a Jew, who seems determined to press us hard. Quite a Shylock, and quite a Patriarch. A picturesque grey-headed and grey-bearded old Jew, in a shovel-hat and gaberdine.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot,‚Äù said Eugene, pausing in setting down his glass, ‚Äúsurely not my worthy friend Mr.¬ÝAaron?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe calls himself Mr.¬ÝRiah.‚Äù

‚ÄúBy the by,‚Äù said Eugene, ‚Äúit comes into my mind that‚ÅÝ‚Äîno doubt with an instinctive desire to receive him into the bosom of our Church‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI gave him the name of Aaron!‚Äù

‚ÄúEugene, Eugene,‚Äù returned Lightwood, ‚Äúyou are more ridiculous than usual. Say what you mean.‚Äù

‚ÄúMerely, my dear fellow, that I have the honour and pleasure of a speaking acquaintance with such a Patriarch as you describe, and that I address him as Mr.¬ÝAaron, because it appears to me Hebraic, expressive, appropriate, and complimentary. Notwithstanding which strong reasons for its being his name, it may not be his name.‚Äù

‚ÄúI believe you are the absurdest man on the face of the earth,‚Äù said Lightwood, laughing.

‚ÄúNot at all, I assure you. Did he mention that he knew me?‚Äù

‚ÄúHe did not. He only said of you that he expected to be paid by you.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhich looks,‚Äù remarked Eugene with much gravity, ‚Äúlike not knowing me. I hope it may not be my worthy friend Mr.¬ÝAaron, for, to tell you the truth, Mortimer, I doubt he may have a prepossession against me. I strongly suspect him of having had a hand in spiriting away Lizzie.‚Äù

‚ÄúEverything,‚Äù returned Lightwood impatiently, ‚Äúseems, by a fatality, to bring us round to Lizzie. ‚ÄòAbout town‚Äô meant about Lizzie, just now, Eugene.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy solicitor, do you know,‚Äù observed Eugene, turning round to the furniture, ‚Äúis a man of infinite discernment!‚Äù

‚ÄúDid it not, Eugene?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes it did, Mortimer.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd yet, Eugene, you know you do not really care for her.‚Äù

Eugene Wrayburn rose, and put his hands in his pockets, and stood with a foot on the fender, indolently rocking his body and looking at the fire. After a prolonged pause, he replied: ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know that. I must ask you not to say that, as if we took it for granted.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut if you do care for her, so much the more should you leave her to herself.‚Äù

Having again paused as before, Eugene said: ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know that, either. But tell me. Did you ever see me take so much trouble about anything, as about this disappearance of hers? I ask, for information.‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear Eugene, I wish I ever had!‚Äù

‚ÄúThen you have not? Just so. You confirm my own impression. Does that look as if I cared for her? I ask, for information.‚Äù

‚ÄúI asked you for information, Eugene,‚Äù said Mortimer reproachfully.

‚ÄúDear boy, I know it, but I can‚Äôt give it. I thirst for information. What do I mean? If my taking so much trouble to recover her does not mean that I care for her, what does it mean? ‚ÄòIf Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled pepper, where‚Äôs the peck, etc.?‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

Though he said this gaily, he said it with a perplexed and inquisitive face, as if he actually did not know what to make of himself. ‚ÄúLook on to the end‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù Lightwood was beginning to remonstrate, when he caught at the words:

‚ÄúAh! See now! That‚Äôs exactly what I am incapable of doing. How very acute you are, Mortimer, in finding my weak place! When we were at school together, I got up my lessons at the last moment, day by day and bit by bit; now we are out in life together, I get up my lessons in the same way. In the present task I have not got beyond this:‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI am bent on finding Lizzie, and I mean to find her, and I will take any means of finding her that offer themselves. Fair means or foul means, are all alike to me. I ask you‚ÅÝ‚Äîfor information‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat does that mean? When I have found her I may ask you‚ÅÝ‚Äîalso for information‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat do I mean now? But it would be premature in this stage, and it‚Äôs not the character of my mind.‚Äù

Lightwood was shaking his head over the air with which his friend held forth thus‚ÅÝ‚Äîan air so whimsically open and argumentative as almost to deprive what he said of the appearance of evasion‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhen a shuffling was heard at the outer door, and then an undecided knock, as though some hand were groping for the knocker. ‚ÄúThe frolicsome youth of the neighbourhood,‚Äù said Eugene, ‚Äúwhom I should be delighted to pitch from this elevation into the churchyard below, without any intermediate ceremonies, have probably turned the lamp out. I am on duty tonight, and will see to the door.‚Äù

His friend had barely had time to recall the unprecedented gleam of determination with which he had spoken of finding this girl, and which had faded out of him with the breath of the spoken words, when Eugene came back, ushering in a most disgraceful shadow of a man, shaking from head to foot, and clothed in shabby grease and smear.

‚ÄúThis interesting gentleman,‚Äù said Eugene, ‚Äúis the son‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe occasionally rather trying son, for he has his failings‚ÅÝ‚Äîof a lady of my acquaintance. My dear Mortimer‚ÅÝ‚ÄîMr.¬ÝDolls.‚Äù Eugene had no idea what his name was, knowing the little dressmaker‚Äôs to be assumed, but presented him with easy confidence under the first appellation that his associations suggested.

‚ÄúI gather, my dear Mortimer,‚Äù pursued Eugene, as Lightwood stared at the obscene visitor, ‚Äúfrom the manner of Mr.¬ÝDolls‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhich is occasionally complicated‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat he desires to make some communication to me. I have mentioned to Mr.¬ÝDolls that you and I are on terms of confidence, and have requested Mr.¬ÝDolls to develop his views here.‚Äù

The wretched object being much embarrassed by holding what remained of his hat, Eugene airily tossed it to the door, and put him down in a chair.

‚ÄúIt will be necessary, I think,‚Äù he observed, ‚Äúto wind up Mr.¬ÝDolls, before anything to any mortal purpose can be got out of him. Brandy, Mr.¬ÝDolls, or‚ÅÝ‚Äî?‚Äù

‚ÄúThreepenn‚Äôorth rum,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝDolls.

A judiciously small quantity of the spirit was given him in a wineglass, and he began to convey it to his mouth, with all kinds of falterings and gyrations on the road.

‚ÄúThe nerves of Mr.¬ÝDolls,‚Äù remarked Eugene to Lightwood, ‚Äúare considerably unstrung. And I deem it on the whole expedient to fumigate Mr.¬ÝDolls.‚Äù

He took the shovel from the grate, sprinkled a few live ashes on it, and from a box on the chimneypiece took a few pastiles, which he set upon them; then, with great composure began placidly waving the shovel in front of Mr.¬ÝDolls, to cut him off from his company.

‚ÄúLord bless my soul, Eugene!‚Äù cried Lightwood, laughing again, ‚Äúwhat a mad fellow you are! Why does this creature come to see you?‚Äù

‚ÄúWe shall hear,‚Äù said Wrayburn, very observant of his face withal. ‚ÄúNow then. Speak out. Don‚Äôt be afraid. State your business, Dolls.‚Äù

‚ÄúMist Wrayburn!‚Äù said the visitor, thickly and huskily. ‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äôtis Mist Wrayburn, ain‚Äôt?‚Äù With a stupid stare.

‚ÄúOf course it is. Look at me. What do you want?‚Äù

Mr.¬ÝDolls collapsed in his chair, and faintly said ‚ÄúThreepenn‚Äôorth rum.‚Äù

‚ÄúWill you do me the favour, my dear Mortimer, to wind up Mr.¬ÝDolls again?‚Äù said Eugene. ‚ÄúI am occupied with the fumigation.‚Äù

A similar quantity was poured into his glass, and he got it to his lips by similar circuitous ways. Having drunk it, Mr.¬ÝDolls, with an evident fear of running down again unless he made haste, proceeded to business.

‚ÄúMist Wrayburn. Tried to nudge you, but you wouldn‚Äôt. You want that drection. You want t‚Äôknow where she lives. Do you Mist Wrayburn?‚Äù

With a glance at his friend, Eugene replied to the question sternly, ‚ÄúI do.‚Äù

‚ÄúI am er man,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝDolls, trying to smite himself on the breast, but bringing his hand to bear upon the vicinity of his eye, ‚Äúer do it. I am er man er do it.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat are you the man to do?‚Äù demanded Eugene, still sternly.

‚ÄúEr give up that drection.‚Äù

‚ÄúHave you got it?‚Äù

With a most laborious attempt at pride and dignity, Mr.¬ÝDolls rolled his head for some time, awakening the highest expectations, and then answered, as if it were the happiest point that could possibly be expected of him: ‚ÄúNo.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat do you mean then?‚Äù

Mr.¬ÝDolls, collapsing in the drowsiest manner after his late intellectual triumph, replied: ‚ÄúThreepenn‚Äôorth rum.‚Äù

‚ÄúWind him up again, my dear Mortimer,‚Äù said Wrayburn; ‚Äúwind him up again.‚Äù

‚ÄúEugene, Eugene,‚Äù urged Lightwood in a low voice, as he complied, ‚Äúcan you stoop to the use of such an instrument as this?‚Äù

‚ÄúI said,‚Äù was the reply, made with that former gleam of determination, ‚Äúthat I would find her out by any means, fair or foul. These are foul, and I‚Äôll take them‚ÅÝ‚Äîif I am not first tempted to break the head of Mr.¬ÝDolls with the fumigator. Can you get the direction? Do you mean that? Speak! If that‚Äôs what you have come for, say how much you want.‚Äù

‚ÄúTen shillings‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThreepenn‚Äôorths Rum,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝDolls.

‚ÄúYou shall have it.‚Äù

‚ÄúFifteen shillings‚ÅÝ‚ÄîThreepenn‚Äôorths Rum,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝDolls, making an attempt to stiffen himself.

‚ÄúYou shall have it. Stop at that. How will you get the direction you talk of?‚Äù

‚ÄúI am er man,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝDolls, with majesty, ‚Äúer get it, sir.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow will you get it, I ask you?‚Äù

‚ÄúI am ill-used vidual,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝDolls. ‚ÄúBlown up morning t‚Äônight. Called names. She makes mint money, sir, and never stands threepenn‚Äôorth rum.‚Äù

‚ÄúGet on,‚Äù rejoined Eugene, tapping his palsied head with the fire-shovel, as it sank on his breast. ‚ÄúWhat comes next?‚Äù

Making a dignified attempt to gather himself together, but, as it were, dropping half a dozen pieces of himself while he tried in vain to pick up one, Mr.¬ÝDolls, swaying his head from side to side, regarded his questioner with what he supposed to be a haughty smile and a scornful glance.

‚ÄúShe looks upon me as mere child, sir. I am not mere child, sir. Man. Man talent. Lerrers pass betwixt ‚Äôem. Postman lerrers. Easy for man talent er get drection, as get his own drection.‚Äù

‚ÄúGet it then,‚Äù said Eugene; adding very heartily under his breath, ‚Äú‚ÅÝ‚ÄîYou brute! Get it, and bring it here to me, and earn the money for sixty threepenn‚Äôorths of rum, and drink them all, one a top of another, and drink yourself dead with all possible expedition.‚Äù The latter clauses of these special instructions he addressed to the fire, as he gave it back the ashes he had taken from it, and replaced the shovel.

Mr.¬ÝDolls now struck out the highly unexpected discovery that he had been insulted by Lightwood, and stated his desire to ‚Äúhave it out with him‚Äù on the spot, and defied him to come on, upon the liberal terms of a sovereign to a halfpenny. Mr.¬ÝDolls then fell a crying, and then exhibited a tendency to fall asleep. This last manifestation as by far the most alarming, by reason of its threatening his prolonged stay on the premises, necessitated vigorous measures. Eugene picked up his worn-out hat with the tongs, clapped it on his head, and, taking him by the collar‚ÅÝ‚Äîall this at arm‚Äôs length‚ÅÝ‚Äîconducted him downstairs and out of the precincts into Fleet Street. There, he turned his face westward, and left him.

When he got back, Lightwood was standing over the fire, brooding in a sufficiently low-spirited manner.

‚ÄúI‚Äôll wash my hands of Mr.¬ÝDolls physically‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù said Eugene, ‚Äúand be with you again directly, Mortimer.‚Äù

‚ÄúI would much prefer,‚Äù retorted Mortimer, ‚Äúyour washing your hands of Mr.¬ÝDolls, morally, Eugene.‚Äù

‚ÄúSo would I,‚Äù said Eugene; ‚Äúbut you see, dear boy, I can‚Äôt do without him.‚Äù

In a minute or two he resumed his chair, as perfectly unconcerned as usual, and rallied his friend on having so narrowly escaped the prowess of their muscular visitor.

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt be amused on this theme,‚Äù said Mortimer, restlessly. ‚ÄúYou can make almost any theme amusing to me, Eugene, but not this.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell!‚Äù cried Eugene, ‚ÄúI am a little ashamed of it myself, and therefore let us change the subject.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is so deplorably underhanded,‚Äù said Mortimer. ‚ÄúIt is so unworthy of you, this setting on of such a shameful scout.‚Äù

‚ÄúWe have changed the subject!‚Äù exclaimed Eugene, airily. ‚ÄúWe have found a new one in that word, scout. Don‚Äôt be like Patience on a mantelpiece frowning at Dolls, but sit down, and I‚Äôll tell you something that you really will find amusing. Take a cigar. Look at this of mine. I light it‚ÅÝ‚Äîdraw one puff‚ÅÝ‚Äîbreathe the smoke out‚ÅÝ‚Äîthere it goes‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs Dolls!‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs gone‚ÅÝ‚Äîand being gone you are a man again.‚Äù

‚ÄúYour subject,‚Äù said Mortimer, after lighting a cigar, and comforting himself with a whiff or two, ‚Äúwas scouts, Eugene.‚Äù

‚ÄúExactly. Isn‚Äôt it droll that I never go out after dark, but I find myself attended, always by one scout, and often by two?‚Äù

Lightwood took his cigar from his lips in surprise, and looked at his friend, as if with a latent suspicion that there must be a jest or hidden meaning in his words.

‚ÄúOn my honour, no,‚Äù said Wrayburn, answering the look and smiling carelessly; ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt wonder at your supposing so, but on my honour, no. I say what I mean. I never go out after dark, but I find myself in the ludicrous situation of being followed and observed at a distance, always by one scout, and often by two.‚Äù

‚ÄúAre you sure, Eugene?‚Äù

‚ÄúSure? My dear boy, they are always the same.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut there‚Äôs no process out against you. The Jews only threaten. They have done nothing. Besides, they know where to find you, and I represent you. Why take the trouble?‚Äù

‚ÄúObserve the legal mind!‚Äù remarked Eugene, turning round to the furniture again, with an air of indolent rapture. ‚ÄúObserve the dyer‚Äôs hand, assimilating itself to what it works in‚ÅÝ‚Äîor would work in, if anybody would give it anything to do. Respected solicitor, it‚Äôs not that. The schoolmaster‚Äôs abroad.‚Äù

‚ÄúThe schoolmaster?‚Äù

‚ÄúAy! Sometimes the schoolmaster and the pupil are both abroad. Why, how soon you rust in my absence! You don‚Äôt understand yet? Those fellows who were here one night. They are the scouts I speak of, as doing me the honour to attend me after dark.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow long has this been going on?‚Äù asked Lightwood, opposing a serious face to the laugh of his friend.

‚ÄúI apprehend it has been going on, ever since a certain person went off. Probably, it had been going on some little time before I noticed it: which would bring it to about that time.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you think they suppose you to have inveigled her away?‚Äù

‚ÄúMy dear Mortimer, you know the absorbing nature of my professional occupations; I really have not had leisure to think about it.‚Äù

‚ÄúHave you asked them what they want? Have you objected?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy should I ask them what they want, dear fellow, when I am indifferent what they want? Why should I express objection, when I don‚Äôt object?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou are in your most reckless mood. But you called the situation just now, a ludicrous one; and most men object to that, even those who are utterly indifferent to everything else.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou charm me, Mortimer, with your reading of my weaknesses. (By the by, that very word, reading, in its critical use, always charms me. An actress‚Äôs reading of a chambermaid, a dancer‚Äôs reading of a hornpipe, a singer‚Äôs reading of a song, a marine painter‚Äôs reading of the sea, the kettledrum‚Äôs reading of an instrumental passage, are phrases ever youthful and delightful.) I was mentioning your perception of my weaknesses. I own to the weakness of objecting to occupy a ludicrous position, and therefore I transfer the position to the scouts.‚Äù

‚ÄúI wish, Eugene, you would speak a little more soberly and plainly, if it were only out of consideration for my feeling less at ease than you do.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen soberly and plainly, Mortimer, I goad the schoolmaster to madness. I make the schoolmaster so ridiculous, and so aware of being made ridiculous, that I see him chafe and fret at every pore when we cross one another. The amiable occupation has been the solace of my life, since I was baulked in the manner unnecessary to recall. I have derived inexpressible comfort from it. I do it thus: I stroll out after dark, stroll a little way, look in at a window and furtively look out for the schoolmaster. Sooner or later, I perceive the schoolmaster on the watch; sometimes accompanied by his hopeful pupil; oftener, pupil-less. Having made sure of his watching me, I tempt him on, all over London. One night I go east, another night north, in a few nights I go all round the compass. Sometimes, I walk; sometimes, I proceed in cabs, draining the pocket of the schoolmaster who then follows in cabs. I study and get up abstruse No Thoroughfares in the course of the day. With Venetian mystery I seek those No Thoroughfares at night, glide into them by means of dark courts, tempt the schoolmaster to follow, turn suddenly, and catch him before he can retreat. Then we face one another, and I pass him as unaware of his existence, and he undergoes grinding torments. Similarly, I walk at a great pace down a short street, rapidly turn the corner, and, getting out of his view, as rapidly turn back. I catch him coming on post, again pass him as unaware of his existence, and again he undergoes grinding torments. Night after night his disappointment is acute, but hope springs eternal in the scholastic breast, and he follows me again tomorrow. Thus I enjoy the pleasures of the chase, and derive great benefit from the healthful exercise. When I do not enjoy the pleasures of the chase, for anything I know he watches at the Temple Gate all night.‚Äù

‚ÄúThis is an extraordinary story,‚Äù observed Lightwood, who had heard it out with serious attention. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt like it.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou are a little hipped, dear fellow,‚Äù said Eugene; ‚Äúyou have been too sedentary. Come and enjoy the pleasures of the chase.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you mean that you believe he is watching now?‚Äù

‚ÄúI have not the slightest doubt he is.‚Äù

‚ÄúHave you seen him tonight?‚Äù

‚ÄúI forgot to look for him when I was last out,‚Äù returned Eugene with the calmest indifference; ‚Äúbut I dare say he was there. Come! Be a British sportsman and enjoy the pleasures of the chase. It will do you good.‚Äù

Lightwood hesitated; but, yielding to his curiosity, rose.

‚ÄúBravo!‚Äù cried Eugene, rising too. ‚ÄúOr, if Yoicks would be in better keeping, consider that I said Yoicks. Look to your feet, Mortimer, for we shall try your boots. When you are ready, I am‚ÅÝ‚Äîneed I say with a Hey Ho Chivey, and likewise with a Hark Forward, Hark Forward, Tantivy?‚Äù

‚ÄúWill nothing make you serious?‚Äù said Mortimer, laughing through his gravity.

‚ÄúI am always serious, but just now I am a little excited by the glorious fact that a southerly wind and a cloudy sky proclaim a hunting evening. Ready? So. We turn out the lamp and shut the door, and take the field.‚Äù

As the two friends passed out of the Temple into the public street, Eugene demanded with a show of courteous patronage in which direction Mortimer would you like the run to be? ‚ÄúThere is a rather difficult country about Bethnal Green,‚Äù said Eugene, ‚Äúand we have not taken in that direction lately. What is your opinion of Bethnal Green?‚Äù Mortimer assented to Bethnal Green, and they turned eastward. ‚ÄúNow, when we come to St.¬ÝPaul‚Äôs churchyard,‚Äù pursued Eugene, ‚Äúwe‚Äôll loiter artfully, and I‚Äôll show you the schoolmaster.‚Äù But, they both saw him, before they got there; alone, and stealing after them in the shadow of the houses, on the opposite side of the way.

‚ÄúGet your wind,‚Äù said Eugene, ‚Äúfor I am off directly. Does it occur to you that the boys of Merry England will begin to deteriorate in an educational light, if this lasts long? The schoolmaster can‚Äôt attend to me and the boys too. Got your wind? I am off!‚Äù

At what a rate he went, to breathe the schoolmaster; and how he then lounged and loitered, to put his patience to another kind of wear; what preposterous ways he took, with no other object on earth than to disappoint and punish him; and how he wore him out by every piece of ingenuity that his eccentric humour could devise; all this Lightwood noted, with a feeling of astonishment that so careless a man could be so wary, and that so idle a man could take so much trouble. At last, far on in the third hour of the pleasures of the chase, when he had brought the poor dogging wretch round again into the City, he twisted Mortimer up a few dark entries, twisted him into a little square court, twisted him sharp round again, and they almost ran against Bradley Headstone.

‚ÄúAnd you see, as I was saying, Mortimer,‚Äù remarked Eugene aloud with the utmost coolness, as though there were no one within hearing by themselves: ‚Äúand you see, as I was saying‚ÅÝ‚Äîundergoing grinding torments.‚Äù

It was not too strong a phrase for the occasion. Looking like the hunted and not the hunter, baffled, worn, with the exhaustion of deferred hope and consuming hate and anger in his face, white-lipped, wild-eyed, draggle-haired, seamed with jealousy and anger, and torturing himself with the conviction that he showed it all and they exulted in it, he went by them in the dark, like a haggard head suspended in the air: so completely did the force of his expression cancel his figure.

Mortimer Lightwood was not an extraordinarily impressible man, but this face impressed him. He spoke of it more than once on the remainder of the way home, and more than once when they got home.

They had been abed in their respective rooms two or three hours, when Eugene was partly awakened by hearing a footstep going about, and was fully awakened by seeing Lightwood standing at his bedside.

‚ÄúNothing wrong, Mortimer?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat fancy takes you, then, for walking about in the night?‚Äù

‚ÄúI am horribly wakeful.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow comes that about, I wonder!‚Äù

‚ÄúEugene, I cannot lose sight of that fellow‚Äôs face.‚Äù

‚ÄúOdd!‚Äù said Eugene with a light laugh, ‚ÄúI can.‚Äù And turned over, and fell asleep again.
